---
title: "SVM & Clustering"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 6 · Lecture A — Maximum-Margin Classification"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Deck setup. Runs from the project root (execute-dir: project), so the shared
# helpers import the same way from any module depth.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "shared")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import slide_helpers as sh
from info521.data import hypertension

sh.use_slide_style()

ds = sh.load_nhanes()
labels, _ = hypertension(ds)
rng = np.random.default_rng(521)          # fixed seed → reproducible figures
```

## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of Lecture A you should be able to:

1. **State the maximum-margin idea** and define **support vectors**.
2. **Compare the hinge loss to the logistic loss** — same boundary family,
   different priorities.
3. **Explain the kernel idea** at concept level — nonlinearity without leaving
   linear machinery.
4. **Evaluate a classifier honestly** — beyond accuracy, at 38.8% prevalence.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Build-fresh per SOURCE_MAP — no Clay source deck for SVM — plus
Clay 23's evaluation thread, compressed). Lecture-only module half; the activity
belongs to Lecture B's clustering.
:::

## One boundary, three philosophies {.smaller}

Module 5 fit $p(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ and took its $\tfrac{1}{2}$-contour as the
boundary. But if **the boundary is all you want**, why model probabilities at all?

- **Logistic** — model the probability; the boundary falls out.
- **Bayes-posterior** — model belief over $\mathbf{w}$; average boundaries.
- **Max-margin** — *skip the probabilities*: choose the boundary directly, by a
  geometric criterion.

[Third act of the course, same linear score $\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}$ —
what changes is **what we optimize**.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: framing slide. Discriminant-only methods trade calibrated
probabilities for directness — a real trade, priced on the eval slide.
:::

## The maximum-margin idea {.smaller}

Many hyperplanes separate the classes. Prefer the one **farthest from both**:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}, b}\ \ \frac{2}{\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert}
\qquad \text{s.t.} \qquad
y_n\left(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n + b\right) \ge 1 \quad \forall n,
\qquad y_n \in \{-1, +1\}
$$

- The **margin** $2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$ is the empty corridor around the
  boundary; maximizing it = minimizing $\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$ — a **norm
  penalty as the *objective***, ridge's cousin (m2b).
- Only the points **on the corridor's edge** constrain the answer — the
  **support vectors**. Delete any other point: nothing moves.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: two through-line hooks — the ‖w‖² objective echoes ridge, and the
sparsity (only support vectors matter) contrasts with least squares, where every
point pulls (m1b projection uses all of y).
:::

## The margin, drawn

```{python}
#| label: fig-margin
#| fig-alt: "Illustrative two-dimensional classification plot with two well-separated point clouds: blue circles lower-left and vermillion squares upper-right. The maximum-margin separating line runs diagonally between them with two dashed margin lines parallel to it; the three points that touch the margin lines — the support vectors — are circled in orange, and the corridor between the dashed lines is lightly shaded."
#| fig-cap: "Maximum margin (illustrative 2-D data): the boundary keeps the widest empty corridor; only the circled support vectors hold it up."

# Illustrative separable clouds (labeled as such): geometry demo, not NHANES.
A = rng.normal([-1.6, -1.2], 0.55, size=(28, 2))
B = rng.normal([1.4, 1.3], 0.55, size=(28, 2))

# Max-margin separator for two round clouds: found by the closest opposite pair
# structure — for this seeded configuration, computed via a tiny grid refinement
# over (angle, offset) maximizing the worst-case signed distance (exact enough
# for the illustration and fully reproducible).
best = None
for th in np.linspace(0, np.pi, 721):
    n = np.array([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])
    pa, pb = A @ n, B @ n
    lo, hi = (pa.max(), pb.min()) if pa.max() < pb.min() else (pb.max(), pa.min())
    if lo < hi:
        m = (hi - lo) / 2
        if best is None or m > best[0]:
            best = (m, n, (lo + hi) / 2)
margin, n_vec, c = best
t_vec = np.array([-n_vec[1], n_vec[0]])

def line(offset, tt):
    p0 = c_pt + offset * n_vec
    return p0[0] + tt * t_vec[0], p0[1] + tt * t_vec[1]

c_pt = c * n_vec
tt = np.linspace(-3.4, 3.4, 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.0, 4.7))
xm, ym = line(-margin, tt)
xp, yp = line(margin, tt)
ax.fill(np.concatenate([xm, xp[::-1]]), np.concatenate([ym, yp[::-1]]),
        color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"], alpha=0.10)
x0, y0 = line(0.0, tt)
ax.plot(x0, y0, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["black"], linestyle="-", alpha=0.65,
        label="max-margin boundary")
ax.plot(xm, ym, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.6)
ax.plot(xp, yp, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.6,
        label="margin edges")
ax.scatter(*A.T, s=30, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], marker="o", label="class −1")
ax.scatter(*B.T, s=30, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], marker="s", label="class +1")
# circle the support vectors (points within numerical reach of the margin edges)
for P in (A, B):
    d = np.abs(P @ n_vec - c)
    sv = P[d <= margin + 0.03]
    ax.scatter(*sv.T, s=190, facecolors="none", edgecolors=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"],
               linewidths=2.0)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$ (illustrative)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$ (illustrative)")
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=10)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Two separable point clouds with the widest-corridor boundary, dashed margin edges, and circled support vectors.")
plt.show()
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: run the deletion thought-experiment on the figure — remove any
uncircled point and the corridor is unchanged; nudge a circled one and the
boundary tilts. That sparsity is the "support" in support vector machine.
:::

## Real data isn't separable: the hinge loss {.smaller}

NHANES ages overlap heavily across hypertension status — no empty corridor
exists. **Soft-margin** SVM lets points buy their way in, at a price:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b}\ \
\underbrace{\sum_{n=1}^{N}\max\!\bigl(0,\ 1 - y_n(\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n + b)\bigr)}_{\text{hinge loss}}
\;+\; \lambda \lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2
$$

- Inside-or-wrong-side points pay **linearly**; comfortable points pay
  **exactly zero**.
- Loss + norm penalty: the ridge template (m2b), with hinge in place of squares.
- $\lambda$ trades margin width against violations — tuned by CV (m2a), as ever.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: everything students know composes: loss + penalty + CV. Only the
loss function is new — and the next slide prices it against logistic.
:::

## Hinge vs. logistic: what each loss cares about

```{python}
#| label: fig-losses
#| fig-alt: "Loss curves as a function of the classification margin y times the score, from minus three to three. The zero-one misclassification loss is a black step at zero; the hinge loss in vermillion is zero beyond margin one and rises linearly leftward with a kink at one; the logistic loss in blue decays smoothly and never quite reaches zero even for confidently correct points."
#| fig-cap: "Three losses vs. the margin y·a: hinge (kinked, exactly 0 past 1) vs. logistic (smooth, never 0) vs. the 0–1 loss both surrogate."

m = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
hinge = np.maximum(0.0, 1.0 - m)
logistic = np.logaddexp(0.0, -m) / np.log(2)      # scaled to pass through 1 at m=0
zero_one = (m < 0).astype(float)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.4, 4.1))
ax.plot(m, zero_one, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["black"], linestyle=":", alpha=0.7,
        drawstyle="steps-post", label="0–1 loss (the real goal)")
ax.plot(m, hinge, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle="-",
        label="hinge (SVM)")
ax.plot(m, logistic, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], linestyle="--",
        label="logistic (M5, rescaled)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"margin  $y \cdot (\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x} + b)$")
ax.set_ylabel("loss")
ax.set_ylim(-0.1, 3.2)
ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False, fontsize=11)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Hinge, logistic, and zero-one loss curves versus classification margin, showing the hinge's flat zero region and the logistic's smooth never-zero decay.")
plt.show()
```

- **Hinge**: ignores comfortable points entirely → *sparse* (support vectors),
  no probabilities.
- **Logistic**: every point always pays a little → calibrated $p(y\mid\mathbf{x})$,
  no sparsity.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the one-figure summary of the philosophical split. Both are convex
stand-ins for the 0–1 loss you actually care about but cannot optimize.
:::

## Kernels, at concept level {.smaller}

<!-- Concept-level by design: no dual derivation, no Mercer theory. -->

Linear boundaries fail when the truth is curved. Old fix (m1b): map to features
$\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ and stay linear *in the weights*.

**Kernel observation**: max-margin training touches inputs only through inner
products — so it needs only

$$
k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')
= \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}')
$$

- Choose $k$ **directly** (polynomial, Gaussian/RBF, …) and never build
  $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ — even when it is infinite-dimensional.
- The boundary becomes a weighted sum of kernels **on the support vectors** —
  flexibility priced per support vector, not per dimension.

[Basis functions (m1b) walked so kernels could run — same move, industrialized.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: keep it honest and shallow: the "kernel trick" is that φ never
needs materializing. If asked for depth, defer to the reading — the dual is out
of scope by design.
:::

## Evaluating classifiers at 38.8% prevalence {.smaller}

NHANES hypertension prevalence is $\approx 0.388$ — so **"nobody is
hypertensive" scores 61% accuracy** while missing every case. Accuracy alone is
a rigged scoreboard.

|  | predicted + | predicted − |
|---|---|---|
| **actually +** | TP | FN *(missed cases — the costly cell)* |
| **actually −** | FP | TN |

- **Sensitivity / recall** $= \mathrm{TP}/(\mathrm{TP}{+}\mathrm{FN})$ — of the
  hypertensive, how many did we catch?
- **Precision** $= \mathrm{TP}/(\mathrm{TP}{+}\mathrm{FP})$ — of our alarms, how
  many were real?
- Margin methods output scores; **where you cut the score** sets the
  sensitivity/precision trade — a *clinical* choice, not a mathematical one.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 23, compressed to the SVM-relevant core; kNN itself is
off-arc — one mention, cut). The 61%-accuracy trap lands hard with the real
prevalence number; make students say which error is worse *for this disease*.
:::

## Recap & the unsupervised turn {.smaller}

- **Max-margin**: widest corridor; held up by **support vectors** only.
- **Hinge vs. logistic**: sparsity + geometry vs. probabilities + calibration —
  same linear score, different contract.
- **Kernels**: inner products only ⇒ nonlinear boundaries with linear machinery.
- **Evaluation**: at 38.8% prevalence, report sensitivity/precision, not accuracy.

[**Next (Lecture B):** drop the labels entirely. What structure lives in the
features alone — and what happens when we *pretend* clusters are
diagnoses?]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this closes the supervised arc begun in m1a. Lecture B's k-means
turn is the course's last conceptual pivot: from predicting y to describing X.
:::
